It used `ramsey.xml`, mw = 3.8759 GHz, programmed det = 1.0 MHz, tau = 0--8 us in 41 points (0.2 us step), snake order, 8 averages x 50,000 repetitions, and tracking per average. The embedded sequence identifies readout 1 as the mS=0 reference and readout 2 as the post-Ramsey signal. ## Calculations/scripts run - Ran `ramsey_analysis.py` with NumPy/SciPy/Matplotlib; outputs are `ramsey_analysis_results.json` and `ramsey_analysis.png`. - Verified the stored combined trace equals the mean of all eight averages to 1.4e-14. Reference and signal means were 49.31 and 44.58 kcps. Their per-average means varied strongly but coherently (reference 40.47--55.53 kcps, signal 36.20--50.27 kcps, correlation 0.9946), while per-average signal/reference means stayed within 0.8909--0.9258. In acquisition order, linear spans were at most 9.43% (reference), 12.57% (signal), and 8.51% (ratio), below the project's prior 15% drift-screen scale. - Linear-detrended Hann FFT: sampling Nyquist 2.5 MHz; bin spacing 121.95 kHz (1/span = 125 kHz). Signal peaks were at 1.220 and 1.098 MHz (0.797 and 0.764 kcps); normalized-ratio peaks were at 1.098 and 1.220 MHz (0.0211 and 0.0188). A dense sinusoid-plus-linear-trend scan selected 1.175 MHz with ratio amplitude 0.02234 (2.47% of mean) and R2 improvement 0.301. After including the 0.2--2.4 MHz frequency search, a residual-permutation test gave p = 0.070. - Robustness was limited: two of eight leave-one-average-out spectra selected 0.445 MHz instead of about 1.175 MHz; the average-bootstrap 95% range was 0.430--1.215 MHz, and only 50% of resamples landed within 125 kHz of 1.175 MHz. - Conditional damped-cosine fits gave T2* = 2.27 us (exponential, f = 1.187 MHz) or 4.76 us (Gaussian, f = 1.181 MHz). Stored-average bootstrap intervals were very broad (exponential 0.18--6.62 us; Gaussian 0.16--8.14 us). Omitting only tau = 0 moved the fits to 4.63 and 5.95 us, respectively. - At the planned carrier (1.000 MHz), planned 13C sidebands (0.615/1.385 MHz), prior scout feature (0.884 MHz), and sidebands around the exploratory 1.175 MHz feature (0.790/1.560 MHz), sinusoid additions all had positive delta-BIC versus a linear trend (+4.3 to +7.1 for the sideband cases), i.e. none was supported. ## Plausible interpretation - This is a technically complete, analyzable Ramsey run with substantial average-to-average common-mode brightness variation that is partly controlled by the reference channel. - A weak Ramsey-like component near 1.18 MHz is plausible: it appears in the signal and normalized FFTs and is not a dominant reference-channel peak. It is nevertheless exploratory, because its global frequency-search significance is marginal and its selected frequency is unstable to average resampling. - The apparent few-microsecond envelope may be real, but the decay is strongly driven by the tau = 0 point and depends on envelope choice. The data therefore suggest a T2* scale of a few microseconds without establishing a defensible value. - No resolved 13C splitting is evident at either the preplanned sidebands or sidebands referenced to the exploratory carrier. ## Claims that are not yet supported - No claim-grade T2* value or uncertainty. - No well-supported carrier assignment at 1.175--1.19 MHz, and no demonstration that the observed frequency follows programmed detuning. The prior weak 0.884 MHz feature at det = 1.5 MHz and the new 1.175 MHz feature at det = 1.0 MHz do not show the expected 0.5 MHz shift. - No evidence for resolvable nearby 13C coupling. Conversely, this SNR/resolution and uncertain carrier do not support claiming absence of nearby 13C. ## Recommended next action Run a same-session detuning-scaling control rather than another identical Ramsey: acquire otherwise matched traces at det = 0.5 and 1.5 MHz, using the refined 3.8759 GHz center and the same tau sampling. Preserve total shots but, if allowed, split them into more averages with fewer repetitions per average to shorten the untracked interval. A genuine Ramsey carrier should shift one-for-one with programmed detuning while fixed-frequency structure indicates artifact/noise. Only after that control identifies a reproducible carrier should a decay fit and sideband search be promoted to T2*/13C conclusions; treat tau = 0 separately in that analysis.

Checks included raw/reference and both normalization views, per-average/acquisition-order drift, least-squares frequency scans, Hann FFT, fixed-frequency projections, and post-hoc decay-fit stress tests with 300 average-resampling trials. - Integrity checks: stored shape is `[8 averages, 2 readouts, 41 tau points]`; the combined traces equal the mean of stored averages to `1.4e-14 kcps`. FFT bin spacing is `125 kHz`, Nyquist is `2.5 MHz`. - No average had a 15% acquisition-order common-mode drop; worst fitted drop was `3.9%`. Inter-average common-mode means nevertheless ranged `38.34..52.90 kcps`, so stored-average agreement is not a clean independent-repeatability test. - A tau-dependent short-time effect is supported: raw signal at `tau=0` is `5.48 +/- 1.04 kcps` below its 2--8 us mean. In paired signal-minus-reference it is `4.93 +/- 0.87 kcps` lower, with the same sign in all 8 averages. - Spectra are analysis-dependent. Full-span raw-signal least squares favors `0.466 MHz` (`0.915 kcps`, partial `R2=0.261`), while point-wise normalization favors `1.178 MHz`; raw signal restricted to `tau>=2 us` favors `1.105 MHz`. The largest Hann raw-signal bins are `1.220` and `1.098 MHz`, followed by `0.488 MHz`. - At the planned frequencies, full-span raw-signal amplitudes are: carrier `1.000 MHz`: `0.277 kcps` (`0.56%` of reference-line level, partial `R2=0.024`); lower 13C position `0.615 MHz`: `0.475 kcps` (`0.96%`, `R2=0.069`); upper 13C position `1.385 MHz`: `0.263 kcps` (`0.53%`, `R2=0.022`). The prior `0.884 MHz` feature is weak (`0.286 kcps`, `R2=0.026`) and is not the dominant new component. - A deliberately post-hoc model consisting of a short exponential transient plus a damped `0.8..1.4 MHz` carrier gives transient time `0.308 us`, carrier `1.168 MHz`, and nominal decay `6.02 us`. It improves BIC over a transient-only model by only `1.36` (weak). Average resampling gives a `2.5..97.5%` decay range of `2.11..100 us`, with 8% at the 100 us fit bound; therefore the nominal decay is not a supported T2star estimate. ## Plausible interpretation The run is valid and contains a repeatable short-tau contrast change. A short pulse/switching transient plus a weak Ramsey-like component near `1.1..1.2 MHz` is plausible and is closer to the programmed `1.0 MHz` carrier than the first scout's `~0.884 MHz` component. However, raw full-span, windowed, and normalized analyses do not select one common frequency, and the transient-plus-carrier decomposition was chosen after seeing the data. The expected pair of 13C sidebands is not resolved; only the lower position has a small projection, without a matching upper feature. ## Claims not yet supported - No claim-grade T2star value. `~6 us` is a model-dependent nominal fit with an effectively unbounded resampling interval. - No nearby-13C coupling assignment, and no claim that 13C is absent. This measurement supports only "no resolved expected sideband pair at the present SNR/model." - No firm assignment of `0.466 MHz`, `1.105..1.220 MHz`, or the old `0.884 MHz` component as the Ramsey carrier or a nuclear sideband. ## Recommended next action Before spending another long run on T2star/13C, perform a controlled det-scaling Ramsey check on r03 with two matched programmed detunings (for example `0.8` and `1.2 MHz`), the same fine-pODMR center, `dt=0.2 us`, balanced snake averages, and either omit `tau<0.4 us` or separately calibrate that short-time transient. Require a raw-signal component with consistent per-average phase to move by the programmed `0.4 MHz`; only then acquire a longer/higher-SNR trace for a T2star envelope and 13C splitting. If the component does not track det, treat the present structure as sequence/apparatus artifact and inspect the Ramsey phase-ramp/switch timing rather than repeating for more shots.

The run completed without abort/stop, with 0–8 us in 41 points, det = 1.0 MHz, 8 averages x 50,000 repetitions, and final counts 44.184 kcps. ## Calculations/scripts run - Created and ran `ramsey_analysis.py`; numerical output is in `ramsey_analysis.json` and the review plot is `ramsey_analysis.png`. - Verified that the combined data equal the mean of the eight stored averages (maximum difference (1.4\times10^{-14}) kcps). Readout 1 is the pre-Ramsey (m_s=0) reference and readout 2 is the Ramsey signal for `full_experiment=0`. - Checked raw signal, point-wise signal/reference, and signal normalized by a fitted reference line; ran line-detrended Hann FFTs, continuous-frequency sinusoid fits, stacked per-average AICc model comparison, forward/reverse-snake and first/last-half fits, and average-resampling bootstraps. - Ran a scan-order-aware within-average common-mode drift check. No average exceeds the pre-existing 15% drop flag. Across averages, reference brightness varies from 40.47 to 55.53 kcps (10.3% CV), but signal and reference means are 0.995 correlated, so most of this is common-mode and pairing the readouts is important. - Fit two-frequency Ramsey models with exponential and Gaussian envelopes and bootstrapped the preferred paired-readout exponential fit. ## Plausible interpretation - This run contains a repeatable, decaying Ramsey signal, materially stronger than the first scout. A two-tone model gives 0.4482 and 1.1716 MHz, midpoint 0.8099 MHz, and full separation 0.7234 MHz. The two-tone model is preferred over the best one-tone model by ΔAICc = -21.6 in the stacked per-average point-wise-ratio analysis. Raw-signal and reference-line-normalized analyses independently prefer the same low/high pair near 0.47/1.17 MHz. - The pair is consistent with ^13C sidebands around a carrier shifted from the programmed 1.0 MHz by residual microwave detuning. The measured half-separation is 0.3617 MHz; a 1,000-resample conditional bootstrap gives 0.3519–0.3874 MHz (95% interval), which includes the model expectation 0.384577 MHz. Constraining the half-split to that expectation gives lines at 0.4304/1.1996 MHz and remains strongly better than one tone (ΔAICc = -17.9), only 3.7 AICc units worse than the free pair. Forward and reverse snake subsets recover low lines at 0.4484/0.4497 MHz and high lines at 1.1671/1.2207 MHz; first/last halves are also compatible at the 0.125 MHz nominal resolution. - Finite few-microsecond dephasing is supported. The paired-readout, two-tone exponential fit gives (T_2^*=3.81) us (average-resampling 95% interval 2.09–8.24 us) and improves AICc by 18.8 over an undamped pair. Normalization/model sensitivity is substantial: exponential fits give 2.74–3.81 us across the three readout treatments, while Gaussian fits give 3.85–4.91 us. The defensible present conclusion is therefore a (T_2^*) on the order of 3–5 us, not a precision value. - The previous approximately 0.884 MHz scout feature is not reproduced as a dominant fixed-frequency component, weakening—but not by itself disproving—the fixed-artifact hypothesis. ## Claims not yet supported - A definitive ^13C assignment is not yet claim-grade from one detuning setting: the splitting is quantitatively consistent, but a stationary technical pair or other spectral structure has not been excluded by a matched detuning-shift control. - An exact (T_2^*), a unique envelope law, or uncertainty narrower than the bootstrap/model spread is not supported. - The programmed 1.0 MHz value is not the observed carrier: the inferred midpoint is about 0.81 MHz. Sub-grid microwave resonance precision and a clean programmed-detuning response are not established. - The first scout's 0.884 MHz component cannot yet be labeled definitively as noise/artifact, because the two runs were not matched in span, averages, or microwave setting. ## Recommended next action Run one matched detuning-shifted Ramsey control on r03: keep 3.8759 GHz, 0–8 us/41 points, 8 x 50,000 shots, tracking/readout handling, and analysis unchanged, but set det = 1.5 MHz. The current interpretation predicts that the pair midpoint should move by the programmed 0.5 MHz in the sequence-defined direction while its full separation stays near (2f_{^{13}C}=0.7692) MHz and the decay remains comparable. That single control best distinguishes a physical ^13C-associated Ramsey pair from stationary technical tones and can tighten the joint (T_2^*) estimate.

The tau=0 ratio is 0.803; its paired deficit relative to tau >= 0.6 us is 0.1046 +/- 0.0171 (mean +/- SEM across 8 averages), with a deficit in every average. - Reordered each average into acquisition order and checked linear common-mode brightness change. None exceeded the pre-existing 15% downward-drift threshold. Average 7 was nevertheless 18.4% dimmer than the across-average median, so between-average brightness variation is material and ratio normalization is important. - For tau >= 0.6 us, scanned coherent sine/cosine least-squares amplitudes over 0.3--2.4 MHz. The maximum is 1.170 MHz with ratio amplitude 0.0173. An exact 256-case average-level sign-flip test gives exploratory scan-wide p=0.0156; the nearby 1.098 MHz component has high phase coherence (0.907). - Checked the planned frequencies. Coherent amplitudes/sign-flip p-values are 0.0113/0.133 at 0.615 MHz, 0.00693/0.188 at 1.000 MHz, 0.00399/0.664 at 1.385 MHz, and 0.00736/0.219 at the prior scout's 0.884 MHz. Sidebands placed +/-0.3846 MHz around the observed 1.170 MHz peak are also small and phase-incoherent. - Fit single damped cosines as sensitivity checks, not estimates: exponential/Gaussian envelopes give T2star=1.14/1.45 us with tau=0 included, but 5.30/6.78 us when only tau=0 is omitted; reduced chi-square remains about 1.5--1.8. This range/shape sensitivity precludes a defensible T2star value. ## Plausible interpretation The run is valid and contains Ramsey-sensitive contrast. The repeatable tau=0 deficit plus the average-coherent 1.10--1.22 MHz band (continuous maximum 1.170 MHz) is plausibly a weak Ramsey carrier close to the programmed 1.0 MHz phase ramp, with a residual frequency offset. The old approximately 0.884 MHz scout feature does not persist. The large tau=0 leverage, competing low-frequency structure, modest amplitude, and unstable envelope fits leave pulse-overlap/initial-point systematics and multi-component or noisy Ramsey behavior unresolved. ## Claims not yet supported - No numerical T2star: the fitted value is dominated by model choice and inclusion of one point. - No detected nearby 13C coupling: neither the predeclared 0.615/1.385 MHz pair nor a pair around the observed carrier is coherent across averages. note_text: # Ramsey review ## Files/data used - `measurement/m001.json`: terminal raw export for `ramsey.xml`; combined data shape `[1,2,41]` and stored-average shape `[1,12,2,41]`. The two readouts were interpreted from the supplied project protocol context as readout 1 = mS=0 reference and readout 2 = Ramsey signal. - `measurement/m002.json` through `measurement/m005.json`: executed settings and terminal provenance. The run used tau = 0.048-1.968 us in 0.048 us steps, `mw_freq = 3.8759 GHz`, programmed `det = 1.0 MHz`, 12 averages x 90,000 repetitions (1.08e6 shots/tau), and snake scan order. It completed without abort, stop request, or reported monitor error; safe shutdown was recorded. Final counts were 35.122 kcps. - `project/brief.md`, `project/state.md`, and `evidence/e017.md`: objective and accumulated r03 interpretation. - `evidence/e001.json` and `evidence/e003.json`: prior det=1.0 MHz, 0-8 us Ramsey raw data/review. Its weak top screen was near 1.178 MHz (ratio amplitude 0.0225), with no T2star/13C claim. - `evidence/e006.json` and `evidence/e009.json`: quantitative design and success criteria for this short-tau diagnostic. ## Calculations/scripts run - Ran `analyze_ramsey.py`, which produced `ramsey_analysis.json` and `ramsey_analysis.png`. It checked raw readouts, point-wise signal/reference, signal normalized by a fitted reference line, between-average SEM, scan-direction/time splits, scan-order-aware common-mode drift, linear least-squares frequency screens, average resampling, and exponential/Gaussian-envelope fits. - Sampling checks: Nyquist = 10.42 MHz, ordinary DFT-bin spacing = 0.508 MHz, and 1/span = 0.521 MHz. Because the FFT bins are coarse, frequency estimates used sinusoidal least squares on a dense grid; they should still be treated as approximate. - The raw signal range is 6.50 kcps versus 2.18 kcps for the reference. Median between-average SEM is 1.14 kcps for raw signal and 0.0127 for the ratio. - Raw signal, point-wise ratio, and fitted-reference-line normalization independently give best frequencies of 1.187, 1.192, and 1.187 MHz. For the mean per-average ratio the best frequency is 1.194 MHz, amplitude 0.0362 (about 0.0724 peak-to-peak), removing 65.7% of residual variance beyond a linear baseline. The raw-signal tone amplitude is 1.68 kcps (3.36 kcps peak-to-peak), within the prior plan's expected order-2-6 kcps peak-to-peak scale. At 1.194 MHz the reference-channel amplitude is only 0.183 kcps. - The first/last six averages give 1.188/1.209 MHz; forward/reverse snake averages give 1.174/1.233 MHz. At 1.194 MHz the coherent per-average ratio amplitude is 0.0362 versus mean individual amplitude 0.0372 (coherence fraction 0.972). No average crosses the supplied 0.15 within-average common-mode drop threshold, although per-average mean brightness varies substantially (reference 42.0-55.2 kcps; signal 37.5-51.2 kcps). - Whole-average resampling is not fully stable: the median best frequency is 1.193 MHz, but 16.8% of resamples select the 0.2 MHz lower screen boundary because a few noisy averages contain strong slow structure. This prevents a precision frequency claim despite the consistent deterministic splits. - Model comparison on the mean per-average ratio favors an undamped tone (AICc -317.65) over exponential- and Gaussian-envelope models (-312.51 and -312.27). The exponential fit returns T2star = 6.73 us but its average-bootstrap interval reaches from about 1.26 us to the 20 us search ceiling; the Gaussian fit is itself at that ceiling, and 73% of its resamples hit it. A finite decay time is therefore not identified by this 1.92 us span. ## Plausible interpretation - This run provides credible evidence for a Ramsey-like, sequence-dependent oscillation near 1.19 MHz. It is present in the raw signal and both normalization views, weak in the reference, phase coherent across averages, robust to first/last and scan-direction splits, and consistent with the weak approximately 1.178 MHz feature in the earlier det=1.0 MHz run. - The approximately 0.19 MHz offset from the programmed 1.0 MHz phase ramp could plausibly reflect a small true-transition/microwave offset, resonance drift during the long acquisition, or a phase/frequency calibration offset. A fixed apparatus transient is less consistent with the signal-only and scan-direction checks, but is not excluded. - The persistence of the oscillation across nearly 2 us argues against the specific very-short/coherence-lost-before-2-us failure mode. The data favor little resolvable decay over this window, not a measured T2star. ## Claims not yet supported - No numerical T2star, including the nominal 6.73 us exponential-fit value; the upper side is unconstrained and the envelope model is not favored over an undamped tone. - No rigorous lower bound on T2star; the apparent persistence is model- and drift-dependent. - No exact carrier frequency or sub-0.1-MHz precision. The short record has coarse Fourier resolution and whole-average resampling exposes low-frequency instability. - No nearby-13C conclusion. The approximately 1.19 MHz component is neither the programmed 1.0 MHz carrier nor the supplied expected 0.615/1.385 MHz sideband positions, and the 1.92 us record cannot establish a resolved carrier/sideband multiplet. - No proof that the 0.19 MHz offset is an NV resonance offset rather than a control/apparatus effect. ## Recommended next action Run one matched, high-SNR short-tau detuning-shift validation on r03, changing only programmed `det` from 1.0 to 1.5 MHz while retaining the 48 ns grid, approximately 2 us span, balanced snake averages, and comparable total shots if current drift/runtime checks allow. A genuine Ramsey component with the same small center offset should shift by approximately +0.50 MHz, from about 1.19 to about 1.69 MHz; a feature that stays near 1.19 MHz or moves inconsistently would favor a sequence/apparatus artifact. Only after det-following is confirmed should a longer, finely sampled high-SNR Ramsey be used to estimate the decay envelope and test for 13C sidebands.

Readout 1 was treated as the true-ms=0 reference and readout 2 as Ramsey signal, following the supplied protocol review. - Checked raw signal, signal/reference, and signal divided by a fitted linear reference line; ran linear-baseline sinusoid screens, a detrended FFT, fixed-frequency tests at 1.000, 0.615, and 1.385 MHz, per-average phase checks, forward/reverse and first/last-half splits, leave-one-average-out tests, scan-order drift slopes, average-level bootstrap, and descriptive exponential-envelope fits/model comparison. Key numbers: - Raw signal peak-to-peak is 6.50 kcps versus 2.18 kcps for the reference; median inter-average SEM is 1.14 kcps (signal) and 0.0127 in signal/reference. - Raw signal and reference-line-normalized screens peak at 1.185 MHz (ratio: 1.190 MHz), with amplitudes 1.68 kcps and 0.0346, respectively. The normalized sinusoid removes 68.1% of residual variance beyond a linear baseline; the reference-only screen removes only 10.0% (nominal p = 0.14). - The 1.000 MHz programmed-carrier component is present but weaker (normalized amplitude 0.0264; 37.8% residual-variance improvement). The new 1.185 MHz peak agrees closely with the prior weak 1.178 MHz exploratory peak under the same nominal detuning. - All 12 per-average phases cluster at 1.185 MHz (circular resultant 0.968). Forward/reverse scan halves peak at 1.175/1.235 MHz and correlate 0.951/0.935 with the combined trace. Leave-one-average-out peaks span only 1.185–1.200 MHz. No average crosses the supplied 15% acquisition-order drift threshold. - Frequency resolution is intrinsically coarse: 1/span = 0.521 MHz and FFT-bin spacing = 0.508 MHz. The detrended FFT has nearly comparable 0.508 and 1.016 MHz bins, so the 1.185 MHz least-squares peak should not be quoted with kHz-scale physical precision. - A descriptive linear-baseline exponential fit gives T2* = 3.06 µs at 1.195 MHz, but the 95% average-bootstrap range is 0.39 µs to the imposed 20 µs bound (15% hit that bound). Adding decay is disfavored relative to a linear-baseline undamped sinusoid (AICc −321.3 versus −325.5), and changing the baseline model moves the fitted decay strongly. ## Plausible interpretation The redesigned acquisition succeeded at its primary diagnostic purpose: r03 now shows a reproducible, readout-specific Ramsey-like oscillation across the full short window. Its stability under scan-direction, time-half, per-average phase, and leave-one-out checks makes ordinary monotonic acquisition drift an implausible sole explanation. The recurrence near 1.18–1.20 MHz in the prior det = 1.0 MHz dataset is additional supporting context. The observed peak is about 0.19 MHz above the programmed 1.0 MHz phase carrier. Plausible explanations include residual microwave-to-transition detuning or a repeatable tau-dependent sequence/background contribution. This dataset alone does not distinguish them. It weakens the specific hypothesis that all prior failures were caused only by a Ramsey decay completed within the first few hundred ns; a structured oscillation persists to about 2 µs. ## Claims that are not yet supported - No numeric T2* is supported. The record does not show identifiable envelope decay independent of the baseline choice, and the fitted uncertainty is effectively open above the measurement window. - No nearby-13C coupling, absence of coupling, or sideband assignment is supported. The expected 0.615/1.385 MHz targets are separated from the 1.0 MHz carrier by only about 0.385 MHz, less than this record's approximately 0.52 MHz resolution, and 1.185 MHz is not uniquely assignable. - The 1.185 MHz value is not a precision resonance-offset measurement and is not yet proven to track the programmed Ramsey detuning. - The short-tau run does not overturn the established r03 alignment evidence, but Ramsey data alone are not new alignment evidence. ## Recommended next action Run one controlled short-tau Ramsey detuning-shift test on the same r03, keeping the tau grid, shot budget, tracking, and readout analysis unchanged while changing only the programmed detuning by a known resolvable amount. It contains two readouts (readout 1 = mS=0 reference; readout 2 = Ramsey signal), 12 stored averages, and snake scan-order metadata. - Acquisition/result checks: `measurement/m002.json` through `measurement/m005.json`. The intended scan completed without abort: tau = 48 ns to 1.968 us in 41 points (48 ns step), `mw_freq = 3.8759 GHz`, programmed `det = 1.0 MHz`, 12 x 90,000 repetitions = 1.08e6 shots per point, final fluorescence 35.122 kcps, and safe shutdown reported true. - Project context: `project/brief.md`, `project/state.md`, `project/advice.md`, `md/memory.md`, and `md/knowledge.md`. - Comparison/design context: `evidence/e003.json` (prior 8 us, det=1.0 MHz terminal review), `evidence/e006.json` (short-tau model), and `evidence/e017.md` (measurement rationale/start). ## Calculations/scripts run - Ran `python analyze_ramsey.py`; it writes `ramsey_analysis.json` and `ramsey_analysis.png`. - Checked raw reference, raw signal, point-wise signal/reference, and signal divided by a fitted reference line. Median across-average point SEM is 1.138 kcps for raw signal and 0.0127 for the ratio. - Fit linear-baseline sine/cosine screens from 0.5 to 2.0 MHz and evaluated the programmed 1.0 MHz carrier plus expected 0.615/1.385 MHz 13C sideband positions. The strongest component is 1.187 MHz in raw signal (1.682 kcps amplitude; 68.1% of linear-baseline residual variance removed), 1.192 MHz in point ratio (0.0363 amplitude; 65.6%), and 1.187 MHz after fitted-reference-line normalization (0.0346 amplitude; 68.1%). The reference channel has only 0.215 kcps maximum screened amplitude and at most 10.0% improvement over the same band. - The new ratio peak agrees with the prior 8 us exploratory peak (1.192 versus 1.178 MHz), while its amplitude is larger (0.0363 versus 0.0225). - Reproducibility checks: up/down snake-direction means correlate at 0.876 in raw signal and 0.780 in ratio; per-average fixed-1-MHz phases have circular resultant 0.933. Scan-order-aware common-mode drift scores flag no averages at the local 0.15 threshold (maximum 0.062). - Average-block bootstrap on the ratio gives a 2.5/50/97.5 percentile free-frequency interval of 1.164/1.196/1.228 MHz and amplitude 0.0301/0.0364/0.0418. This is an empirical stability check, not a full systematic uncertainty. - Decay-model check: a free-frequency exponential fit returns a descriptive T2star near 3.00 us, but its AICc is 1.51 worse than an undamped sinusoid and its profile interval runs from about 0.86 us to the imposed 100 us upper limit. A Gaussian envelope runs to the 100 us boundary and is also worse. Therefore the decay is not identified. - FFT bin spacing is 0.508 MHz (1/span = 0.521 MHz); the coarse FFT is edge/leakage sensitive and cannot distinguish 1.0 from about 1.19 MHz or resolve the expected sidebands separated by about 0.385 MHz. It was used only as a sampling/resolution check. ## Plausible interpretation The new data support a reproducible early-time Ramsey-like oscillation on r03. It is much stronger in the signal than the reference, persists in both snake directions with coherent phase, and repeats the approximately 1.18 MHz feature seen weakly in the prior det=1.0 MHz run. Simple within-average drift is therefore not a good explanation. The specific hypothesis that all useful coherence vanished well before 2 us is disfavored. The observed component is about 0.19 MHz above the programmed 1.0 MHz phase ramp. Resonance/calibration drift, sequence-dependent phase/detuning behavior, or unresolved spectral structure are plausible, but this dataset does not discriminate among them. ## Claims not yet supported - No numeric T2star is supported. Checked raw channels, point-wise signal/reference, signal divided by a fitted reference line, per-point SEM, all 12 stored averages, forward/reverse snake averages, acquisition-order brightness slopes, least-squares frequency scans, unpadded FFTs, residual-permutation screens, and exponential/Gaussian/undamped model comparisons. Also compared and jointly screened the new trace with the prior det=1.0 MHz data. - New acquisition: tau 0.048-1.968 us, 48 ns step, 41 points, 12 x 90,000 = 1.08e6 shots/point. It completed without abort, stop, or monitor error. Final count was 35.122 kcps, 20.5% below the prior run's 44.184 kcps but above the 20 kcps gate. - New raw means are 48.57 kcps reference and 44.65 kcps signal. The signal varies more over tau than the reference (SD 1.57 vs 0.46 kcps); median signal SEM is 1.14 kcps, 0.593 times the prior run's value. No average exceeds the 15% acquisition-order drop threshold (largest downward score 6.0%). - A free-frequency sinusoid plus linear baseline gives about 1.20 MHz and 3.43% ratio amplitude. The exact frequency is not resolved: the 1.92 us span gives 0.521 MHz nominal resolution (0.508 MHz FFT bins) and only 1.92 programmed-carrier cycles. Forward/reverse detrended traces correlate at 0.750 and their fixed-1 MHz phases differ by only 0.003 rad. The new and prior det=1.0 MHz traces correlate at 0.894 over their shared 0.2-1.968 us interval; the prior long-window FFT also has a leading bin at 1.220 MHz. - Decay inference is unstable. On the new ratio, undamped, exponential, and Gaussian fits give AICc 44.66, 44.72, and 47.28; the decay fits return 2.09 and 4.31 us. A joint old/new fit returns 3.19 us exponential or 6.61 us Gaussian, with only weak model preference. Forcing the frequency to exactly 1.0 MHz instead produces a misleading 0.196 us fit, showing why that number must not be promoted. ## Plausible interpretation The short-tau/high-SNR run succeeds at its diagnostic purpose: it reveals a reproducible, readout-specific carrier-like Ramsey oscillation near 1.2 MHz and makes a very-short (roughly <=0.6 us) decay less plausible as the reason earlier full-window analyses failed. Agreement across snake directions and with the independent early-time portion of the prior det=1.0 MHz run argues against a simple scan-direction drift artifact. However, the roughly +0.2 MHz offset from the programmed 1.0 MHz carrier, the previous det=1.5 MHz run's failure to show a consistent det-following feature, and the late-run count loss leave resonance drift or a sequence/timing artifact as viable alternatives. ## Claims that are not yet supported - No claim-grade numeric T2star: the window does not establish an envelope, and fitted values are strongly model/frequency dependent. - No nearby-13C presence, absence, or coupling claim: the 0.521 MHz resolution is worse than the expected 0.385 MHz carrier-sideband separation, so the 0.615, 1.000, and 1.385 MHz components are not separable in this run. - Do not claim 1.2005 MHz precision, a 0.196 us T2star, or that the feature is conclusively physical rather than control/timing related. ## Recommended next action After retracking and rechecking the resonance because counts fell 20.5%, run one same-grid, same-SNR short-tau Ramsey with a deliberately shifted detuning (preferably 2.0 MHz, giving a resolvable +1.0 MHz test). Require the oscillation to shift by the programmed amount while retaining a compatible envelope. If it does, acquire a longer high-SNR/phase-cycled or quadrature Ramsey with enough span to fit the decay and resolve the approximately 0.385 MHz 13C splitting; if it does not, stop blind Ramsey repeats and investigate the sequence/timing path.

note_text: # Ramsey review — r03 short-tau diagnostic ## Files/data used - New terminal run: `measurement/m001.json` (raw reference/signal arrays, 12 stored averages, errors, and snake order), `m002.json` (requested settings), `m003.json` (terminal result), and `m004.json`/`m005.json` (status/control). - Project context: `project/brief.md`, `project/state.md`, `project/advice.md`, `md/memory.md`, and `md/knowledge.md`. - Prior Ramsey context: `evidence/e001.json` (raw 0–8 us run), `e002.json`/`e003.json` (its drift and terminal review), and `e006.json`/`e017.md` (rationale for the new diagnostic). ## Calculations/scripts run - Ran `ramsey_analysis.py`; machine-readable results are in `ramsey_analysis_summary.json` and the checked plot is `ramsey_diagnostic.png`. - Reconstructed raw signal/reference, point-wise signal/reference, and signal divided by a fitted linear reference. Used linear-baseline plus sine/cosine least-squares screens, FFT-bin/resolution checks, forward/reverse snake splits, per-average phase coherence, scan-order drift slopes, stored-average bootstrap, and exponential/Gaussian descriptive decay fits. The earlier run's known tau=0 artifact was excluded in cross-run fits. - New acquisition: 48 ns–1.968 us in 48 ns steps (41 points), 12 × 90,000 = 1.08 million shots/point; completed without abort/stop/monitor error, final counts 35.122 kcps. - The oscillation is present in raw and normalized views: best free screen is 1.192 MHz in signal/reference (amplitude 0.0363; 65.6% residual-variance reduction over a linear baseline) and 1.187 MHz in raw signal (1.682 kcps; 68.1% reduction). Fitted-reference normalization gives amplitude 0.0346, while the raw reference has only 0.183 kcps amplitude at 1.192 MHz. Median across-average SEM is 1.138 kcps raw and 0.0127 in ratio. - Reproducibility checks support signal presence: phase-coherence ratio across all 12 averages is 0.972; forward/reverse halves independently peak at 1.172/1.232 MHz with ratio amplitudes 0.0385/0.0356. No average crosses the 15% within-average drift threshold (largest score 6.2%), although average-to-average common brightness changes are substantial (minimum/maximum 0.747), so raw/reference-aware treatment remains necessary. - Cross-run check: after removing tau=0, the prior 8 us run's free screen is 1.188 MHz. Its first 2 us has ratio amplitude 0.0347 and phase 2.532 rad at the new frequency, close to the new run's 0.0363 and 2.314 rad. This makes the earlier weak ~1.18 MHz component credible as an early-time Ramsey component. - Decay is not stable. Short-run exponential-bootstrap T2* has median 5.45 us but 35.8% of resamples hit the 100 us upper bound. A joint descriptive fit gives 4.67 us (exponential) or 6.05 us (Gaussian), with 95% stored-average bootstrap ranges of 1.41–78.6 us and 1.86–15.7 us, respectively. ## Plausible interpretation - The redesigned run succeeds at its main diagnostic purpose: r03 has a real, repeatable early-time Ramsey oscillation under the current sequence. A descriptive effective frequency near 1.19 MHz is consistent across the new data, snake directions, and the early portion of the prior run. - A T2* on the order of a few microseconds is plausible, but the present records do not determine it robustly: the new window is shorter than most fitted decay times, while the older long-window data are much noisier and coarser at early tau. - The 1.19 MHz value may reflect the effective Ramsey carrier under this implementation, but it should not yet be promoted to an exact resonance-frequency correction. ## Claims not yet supported - No claim-grade numeric T2*. The fitted value and uncertainty depend strongly on envelope model, weighting, and stored-average resampling. - Neither nearby-13C presence nor absence. The new 1.920 us span has nominal resolution 0.521 MHz (FFT bins 0.508 MHz), worse than the expected ~0.385 MHz carrier-to-sideband separation and shorter than one corresponding beat period. Fixed screens at 0.615, 1.000, and 1.385 MHz are therefore strongly correlated, not separate peaks. - No sub-resolution claim that the physical carrier is exactly 1.192 MHz or that its ~0.19 MHz offset from the programmed 1.0 MHz phase ramp is a calibrated detuning error. ## Recommended next action Design and advisory-check one targeted long-and-fine Ramsey acquisition on the same r03: preferably about 8 us span with ≤0.1 us spacing, nonzero first tau, even averages, and enough total shots to retain approximately the new run's ratio SEM (about 0.013). Reduce repetitions per average and increase tracked averages as needed to keep the per-average tracking window within the active cap. This combines the new run's early-time resolution with ~0.125 MHz spectral resolution, allowing a constrained T2* envelope and separable carrier/±13C-sideband test. Predefine raw/reference-aware, direction-split, and bootstrap acceptance checks; do not repeat either existing grid unchanged.

note_text: # Ramsey review — r03 short-tau/high-SNR diagnostic ## Files/data used - New measurement: `measurement/m001.json` (terminal raw export), `measurement/m002.json` (job/configuration), `measurement/m003.json` (completed result), `measurement/m004.json` (terminal status), and `measurement/m005.json` (no-stop control). - Project context: `project/brief.md`, `project/advice.md`, `project/state.md`, `context.json`, and the Ramsey/readout/drift guidance in `md/memory.md` and `md/knowledge.md`. - Directly relevant prior evidence: `evidence/e001.json`–`evidence/e006.json` (especially the prior 8 us Ramsey terminal review and short-tau model plan), `evidence/e009.json` (verified intent/success criteria), and `evidence/e017.md` (run design/start note). No external data or literature were used. ## Calculations or scripts run - Wrote and ran `ramsey_analysis.py`; it produced `ramsey_analysis_summary.json` and `ramsey_diagnostic.png`. The script independently parsed both readouts, checked that the mean of 12 stored averages reproduces the combined trace (maximum difference `1.4e-14 kcps`), computed raw signal, point-wise signal/reference, and signal/fitted-reference-line views, and evaluated per-point SEM. - Confirmed the acquisition: `tau=0.048..1.968 us`, 41 points at 48 ns spacing, `det=1.0 MHz`, 12 averages x 90,000 repetitions = `1.08e6` shots/tau. Mean reference and signal were `48.573` and `44.655 kcps`; median signal SEM was `1.138 kcps`, close to the planned `1.167 kcps`. The signal trace spans `6.499 kcps` peak-to-peak. - Ran scan-order-aware common-mode drift checks using the recorded snake order. No average crossed the project's 15% downward-drift threshold; the largest downward fitted change was `6.2%` (average 10). Counts did vary between averages (reference means `42.016..55.188 kcps`) and the terminal tracker count was `35.122 kcps`, but the run stayed above the 20 kcps gate, completed without abort/error, and reported safe shutdown. - Ran linear-baseline plus sinusoid screens, detrended/Hann FFT, per-average phase checks, ascending/descending snake splits, leave-one-average-out checks, and 4,000 resamples of the 12 averages. At the programmed 1.0 MHz, amplitudes were `1.282 kcps` in raw signal, `0.0272` in signal/reference, and `0.0264` in signal/fitted-reference-line. The per-average 1 MHz phases were coherent (circular resultant `0.948`; descriptive 2-D coefficient test `p≈4e-16`), and the ratio-amplitude bootstrap interval was `0.0212..0.0333` (2.5–97.5%). - The best single-sinusoid screen was `1.194 MHz` unweighted and `1.202 MHz` SEM-weighted, with raw-signal amplitude about `1.68 kcps` and ratio amplitude about `0.036`. Leave-one-average-out optima were `1.188..1.202 MHz`; ascending/descending subsets gave `1.174/1.232 MHz`. The FFT is necessarily coarse (`0.508 MHz` bin spacing; `1/span=0.521 MHz`) and placed its largest bins at `1.016` and `1.524 MHz`, consistent with leakage around a carrier-band feature rather than resolved lines. - Tested exponential and Gaussian damped-cosine models only after establishing signal presence. Free-frequency fits gave `T2*=2.15 us` (exponential) versus `4.53 us` (Gaussian), with reduced chi-square `2.44` and `2.59`; local uncertainties were `1.12` and `5.84 us`. A profile check gave an exponential delta-chi-square-3.84 range of about `1.18..12.9 us`, while the Gaussian upper range remained open through the 100 us test bound. Fixed-1-MHz resampling was also unstable (`0.143 us` to the 100 us bound at the 2.5–97.5% percentiles). These are diagnostics of non-identifiability, not T2* results. ## Plausible interpretation - This redesigned run succeeds at its narrow purpose: r03 has reproducible early-time Ramsey contrast. The agreement of raw signal, both normalization views, both snake directions, and per-average phase argues against the feature being created solely by the reference denominator or a monotonic scan drift. This is a meaningful improvement over the two prior non-claim-grade Ramsey datasets. - The dominant modulation is in the expected carrier band but its simple-sinusoid optimum is near `1.20 MHz`, not exactly the programmed `1.0 MHz`. Residual microwave/resonance detuning or a more complicated multi-frequency/baseline structure are both plausible; the two-cycle window does not distinguish them. - The trace does not show a clean, model-independent decay envelope. A coherence time of order a few microseconds is plausible under a single-component model, but the present data do not select that model or bound its decay time robustly. The earlier “no visible carrier because T2* is extremely short” failure mode is disfavored by contrast persisting across the 1.92 us window. - The aligned-r03 conclusion remains supported by the prior pODMR evidence; this Ramsey run adds coherence-signal evidence but is not itself a new alignment measurement. ## Claims not yet supported - No numerical `T2*` claim: optimizer convergence and the `2.15/4.53 us` descriptive fits are model-dependent, have poor residual statistics, and have broad/open profile ranges. - No exact Ramsey/carrier frequency or `~0.20 MHz` residual-detuning claim: only about two cycles were measured and the fit model is imperfect. - No positive or negative nearby-`13C` conclusion. The expected sidebands from the project model (`0.615` and `1.385 MHz` for a 1.0 MHz carrier) are separated from the carrier by about `0.385 MHz`, smaller than this run's `0.521 MHz` nominal resolution; their target-fit amplitudes are therefore strongly covariant. There is no resolved, repeatable sideband pair, and absence cannot be tested with this short window. - No assignment of the non-monotonic envelope to `13C`, another nuclear spin, hyperfine beating, or an instrumental artifact. ## Recommended next action Do not repeat this identical short scan or promote one decay fit. After a fresh TrackCenter and weak-pi pODMR center check (counts ended lower and multi-hour resonance drift is plausible in the local context), run a phase-cycled/two-quadrature Ramsey that keeps the now-demonstrated shot quality but extends the span to about 8 us. A concrete starting grid is `0.048..8.0 us` with about 51 points (Nyquist about `3.14 MHz`, nominal resolution about `0.126 MHz`) and about `1e6` shots per point, split into more even stored averages and fewer repetitions per average (for example `16 x 60k`) so the current tracking-window cap can be rechecked and respected. Jointly fit the quadratures with shared carrier/decay parameters. Accept T2* only if the envelope and residuals agree across decay models/splits; test `13C` using reproducible peaks at the measured carrier ± the project-predicted `~0.385 MHz`, rather than assuming the carrier is exactly 1.0 MHz.

note_text: # Ramsey review ## Handoff result The detuning-shift run completed cleanly and is analyzable, but it is not claim-grade for a Ramsey carrier, T2star, or nearby 13C. The data disfavor the *exact fixed* 1.192 MHz feature as the sole explanation: the full-record signal/reference screen moves from 1.192 MHz at det = 1.0 MHz to 1.623 MHz at det = 1.5 MHz (observed shift +0.431 MHz versus +0.500 MHz predicted). However, that partial tracking evidence is not robust across readout views or time-window checks. Raw signal and signal/fitted-reference-line both peak at 0.882 MHz, the reference itself peaks at 0.924 MHz, and removing tau <= 0.2 us moves the new ratio/raw-signal tops to 0.746/0.806 MHz. A common tau-dependent transient plus a weaker detuning-sensitive contribution is plausible, but the present data do not separate them reliably. ## Files/data used - `measurement/m001.json`: terminal raw export, including combined and 12 per-average reference/signal traces and snake acquisition order. - `measurement/m002.json`–`m005.json`: intended configuration and terminal job/result/status/control. The run used `mw_freq = 3.8759 GHz`, `det = 1.5 MHz`, tau = 48 ns–1.968 us in 41 points, and 12 averages x 90,000 repetitions; it completed with final counts 44.796 kcps, no stop request/error, and safe shutdown. - `evidence/e006.json`, `evidence/e007.json`, and `evidence/e008.json`: terminal det = 1.0 MHz short-tau control, its drift result, and prior interpretation. - `evidence/e019.json` and `evidence/e021.json`: the predeclared det-shift hypotheses and frequency targets (1.500 MHz programmed carrier, 1.692 MHz tracked-carrier prediction, 1.192 MHz fixed control, and tracked 13C targets near 1.307/2.077 MHz). - `project/brief.md`, `project/state.md`, and `project/advice.md`: objective and prior r03 spectroscopy/Ramsey context. ## Calculations/scripts run - Created and ran `analyze_ramsey.py`; reproducible results are in `ramsey_analysis.json` and `ramsey_analysis.png`. - Verified the `[average, readout, tau]` axis contract by reconstructing combined data from per-average traces (maximum discrepancy `1.42e-14 kcps`). Sampling gives a 1.920 us span, nominal `1/span = 0.521 MHz` resolution, 0.508 MHz FFT-bin spacing, and 10.417 MHz Nyquist frequency; dense-screen frequencies therefore do not imply kHz-scale precision. - Ran raw-signal, point-wise signal/reference, and signal/fitted-reference-line checks; linear-detrended FFT and sinusoidal least-squares screens; full and `tau > 0.2 us` screens; target-frequency amplitudes; per-average/leave-one-out checks; 1,000 average-bootstrap resamples; and a descriptive damped-sinusoid grid fit. - Reproduced the documented scan-order common-mode drift check at a 15% drop threshold. No averages were flagged; fitted end-to-start changes ranged from -4.04% to +7.36%. Median between-average point SEM was 0.711 kcps for raw signal and 0.0126 for per-average ratios. Key full-record least-squares results (each includes a linear baseline): | View | det = 1.0 MHz top | det = 1.5 MHz top | |---|---:|---:| | Raw signal | 1.187 MHz, 1.682 kcps | 0.882 MHz, 1.533 kcps | | Signal/reference | 1.192 MHz, 0.0363 | 1.623 MHz, 0.02547 | | Signal/fitted reference line | 1.187 MHz, 0.03461 | 0.882 MHz, 0.03193 | | Raw reference | 1.019 MHz | 0.924 MHz | At the new run's predicted 1.692 MHz carrier, amplitudes/R2 gains over a linear baseline are `1.225 kcps/0.427` (raw) and `0.02505/0.411` (ratio), versus `0.474 kcps/0.064` and `0.00511/0.017` at the fixed 1.192 MHz control. Restricted bootstrap comparisons choose 1.692 MHz over 1.192 MHz in 100% of resamples, but this does not test the stronger competing ~0.88 MHz transient and preserves systematic tau structure. Leave-one-average-out ratio tops range from 0.839 to 1.640 MHz. The ratio FFT maximum is the coarse 1.524 MHz bin and cannot resolve 1.500 from 1.692 MHz. ## Plausible interpretation - The measurement itself is healthy; count collapse and within-average scan drift do not explain the result. - The old component is not simply fixed at 1.192 MHz after the +0.5 MHz det change. The full-record ratio contains partial evidence for a detuning-sensitive component in the expected broad frequency region. - The stronger readout-view and window dependence indicates at least one superposed tau-dependent baseline/protocol transient near 0.9 MHz. The full-record ratio likely suppresses or reshapes this common component, so its 1.623 MHz maximum cannot yet be promoted as the physical Ramsey carrier. - Descriptive damped fits select inconsistent non-carrier solutions (raw: 0.818 MHz, 0.717 us; ratio: 0.678 MHz, 0.469 us), confirming that a numeric decay time is model/transient sensitive rather than a supported T2star. ## Claims not yet supported - A clean programmed- or detuning-tracked Ramsey carrier on r03. - Any numeric T2star. The fit precondition—consistent carrier/decay behavior across raw and readout-aware views—is not met. - Nearby 13C coupling or its absence. The tracked sideband targets at 1.307/2.077 MHz are weak and non-dominant (ratio amplitudes 0.00953/0.00614 with R2 gains 0.056/0.023; raw amplitudes 0.268/0.251 kcps with R2 gains 0.020/0.017), with no supported symmetric carrier/sideband model. - Greater frequency precision than the short record supports. The 1 kHz LS grid is an interpolation screen, not 1 kHz experimental resolution. The prior strong-/weak-pi pODMR evidence for r03 as an aligned, resonant candidate remains supported; this Ramsey run does not invalidate it. ## Recommended next action Do not repeat the same Ramsey sequence. First validate an alternate phase-cycled/differential Ramsey protocol on r03 (opposite final-pulse phases, with the difference channel used to cancel the tau-dependent optical/reference transient), then compare det = 1.0 and 1.5 MHz under otherwise matched conditions. Require the differential raw signal to shift by about +0.5 MHz consistently across readout views before extending the tau window for T2star and 13C FFT analysis. If a phase-cycled route is unavailable, close the present r03 Ramsey branch as T2star/13C unsupported under this protocol rather than accumulating another blind repeat.

note_text: # Ramsey review ## Files/data used - New terminal run: `measurement/m001.json` (raw savedexperiment export), `measurement/m002.json` (programmed settings), and `measurement/m003.json` through `m005.json` (completion, safety, status, and control provenance). - Direct comparison: `evidence/e006.json` (terminal det = 1.0 MHz short-tau raw export), `evidence/e008.json` (its prior review), `evidence/e019.json` (prespecified det-shift model), and `evidence/e007.json`/`e009.py` (prior drift/analysis contracts). - Project context: `project/brief.md`, `project/state.md`, `project/advice.md`, `md/memory.md`, and `md/knowledge.md`. The common `ramsey.xml`, with `full_experiment = 0`, assigns readout 1 as reference and readout 2 as Ramsey signal in the provided review context. ## Calculations/scripts run - Created and ran `ramsey_quant_check.py`; outputs are `ramsey_quant_results.json` and `ramsey_quant_check.png`. - Verified the raw axis contract `[scan, average, readout, tau]`: averaging the 12 stored averages reconstructs both combined readouts to maximum absolute error `1.4e-14`. - Checked raw signal, raw reference, point-wise signal/reference, signal divided by a fitted reference line, per-point SEM, snake-order within-average drift, FFT/least-squares frequency content, early-point exclusion, leave-one-average-out stability, 200 average-resampling screens, joint det-tracking versus fixed-frequency models, and descriptive damped-sinusoid grids. ## Quantitative result and plausible interpretation - The run completed normally: 12 averages x 90,000 repetitions = 1.08 million shots per tau point, tau `0.048..1.968 us` in 48 ns steps, final count `44.796 kcps`, no abort/stop, safe shutdown OK, and empty monitor error. The scan-order-aware check flags no average; the largest fitted within-average readout drop is `5.35%`, below the existing `15%` flag threshold. - New-run mean reference/signal are `48.084/44.269 kcps`. Median SEM is `0.711 kcps` for raw signal and `0.0126` for the per-average ratio. The early (`tau <= 0.75 us`) excursion is large (`6.46 kcps` raw and `0.134` ratio), but its spectral interpretation is view-dependent. - Point-wise ratio: the all-tau least-squares screen peaks at `1.623 MHz` (amplitude `0.0255`, improvement over a linear baseline `R2 = 0.430`). This is near the prespecified `1.692 MHz` det-tracking prediction; at `1.692 MHz`, amplitude is `0.0250` and `R2 = 0.411`. The old fixed `1.192 MHz` feature is nearly absent (`0.0051`, `R2 = 0.017`). A joint ratio diagnostic favors a common detuning offset (`1.18/1.68 MHz`; BIC-like score `251.9`) over a frequency fixed across runs (`1.19 MHz`; `320.1`), and is essentially tied with independent frequencies (`251.2`). This argues against a strictly det-independent 1.19 MHz baseline feature. - Raw-readout consistency fails, however. The new raw signal and signal/fitted-reference-line screens both peak at `0.882 MHz`, not near the ratio peak. The raw `1.692 MHz` component is present but subdominant (amplitude `1.225 kcps`, `R2 = 0.427`) to the raw `0.882 MHz` screen maximum (`1.533 kcps`, `R2 = 0.577`). The joint raw-signal diagnostic favors unrelated run frequencies (`1.174/0.882 MHz`; score `129.0`) over det tracking (`1.154/1.654 MHz`; `152.8`). Thus the apparent det shift is primarily a point-wise-normalization result, not a readout-consistent Ramsey carrier. - Stability is also weak: after removing `tau <= 0.2 us`, the ratio maximum moves from `1.623` to `0.746 MHz`; three of 12 leave-one-average-out screens move to `0.84..0.856 MHz`; and average-resampling gives a broad `16..84%` range of `0.844..1.660 MHz`, with only `57%` of resamples above `1.45 MHz`. - Descriptive damped fits disagree: ratio gives approximately `0.678 MHz / T2* 0.469 us`, while raw signal gives `0.818 MHz / T2* 0.717 us`. These also conflict with the prior run's descriptive values and with the undamped screen, so the fit improvements do not identify a robust physical decay. - Plausible interpretation: the det change affects the observed early-time structure, so a det-dependent Ramsey response or sequence/control transient remains possible and a fixed 1.19 MHz artifact is disfavored. Reference-denominator effects and short-lived/non-sinusoidal transients remain equally important because raw and normalized views disagree. The independently established r03 aligned-NV conclusion remains supported by the prior pODMR evidence; this run does not strengthen the Ramsey/T2*/13C conclusion. ## Claims not yet supported - No numeric `T2*` is supported; the fitted value is strongly view-, window-, and model-dependent. - No physical carrier at `1.623`, `1.68`, or `1.692 MHz` is established because it is not the dominant raw-signal feature and is unstable to early-point/average checks. - Neither nearby-13C coupling nor absence of nearby 13C is supported. The expected det = 1.5 MHz sidebands (`1.115` and `1.885 MHz`) are not distinct or readout-consistent, and their `0.385 MHz` carrier separation is smaller than this window's nominal `0.521 MHz` spectral resolution. - The data do not distinguish genuine short-lived NV coherence from a det-dependent pulse-sequence/control transient. ## Recommended next action Do not fit or repeat this same short-tau protocol. First run a phase-cycled/quadrature Ramsey control on r03 with det = 1.0 and 1.5 MHz interleaved under the same tracking conditions, and use the raw phase-difference signal as the primary observable. Require a raw, phase-reversing carrier that shifts by about `0.5 MHz` and survives early-point exclusion and stored-average checks before extracting `T2*`. Only after that gate passes should the tau span be extended enough to resolve the `~0.385 MHz` 13C splitting (nominally greater than `2.6 us`, preferably with margin); if the gate fails, close the current r03 Ramsey protocol as unable to support T2*/13C claims rather than accumulating another identical run.

It verified the array contract (`[20 averages, 2 readouts, 41 tau points]`, whose mean equals the stored combined data), readout roles, scan sampling, SEM, raw/point-wise/fitted-reference views, full/skip-first-four frequency screens, per-average spectra, and snake-direction/common-mode checks. - `ramsey_model_fit.py` produced `ramsey_model_fit_metrics.json`, `ramsey_model_fit.png`, and `ramsey_model_fit_run.log`. It compared linear-baseline single-tone and two-tone exponential Ramsey models, including a pair constrained to separation `2*f_13C = 0.76961 MHz`, and performed stored-average block bootstraps. - Scan checks: tau = 0.048-8.048 us, 0.200 us step, 41 points, 20 x 50,000 = 1.0e6 shots/tau, nominal resolution 0.125 MHz, Nyquist 2.5 MHz. Median combined-signal SEM was 0.850 kcps. - Drift/readout checks: forward-vs-reverse snake means differed by an RMS of 1.00 pooled-SEM units. Average-level reference and signal counts varied substantially but were strongly common-mode (correlation 0.970); raw signal and fitted-reference normalization gave nearly identical primary fits. - Full-span raw fit: the free two-tone model found 1.528 and 2.265 MHz, separation 0.737 MHz, with common exponential decay 3.06 us. After skipping the first four points it found 1.517 and 2.262 MHz, separation 0.745 MHz, decay 2.91 us; the two-tone advantage remained large (Delta-BIC about 28). - The 13C-constrained raw model (1.511 and 2.281 MHz about a fitted 1.896 MHz center) fit essentially as well as the free pair: BIC -55.89 and residual RMS 0.352 kcps, below the 0.850 kcps median raw SEM. The same fitted-reference result was 2.95 us. - A 500-block bootstrap of the constrained model gave T2star = 2.99 us (raw 95% interval 2.38-3.69 us) and 2.93 us (fitted-reference 2.40-3.95 us). A 400-block unconstrained ratio bootstrap gave pair separation 0.733 MHz (95% interval 0.674-0.786 MHz), which contains the expected 0.7696 MHz. Both fitted components were phase-aligned in 20/20 stored averages (phase-resultant lengths 0.81 and 0.88). ## Plausible interpretation - This run contains a real, decaying, two-component Ramsey signal rather than the weak/mixed single-frequency screens seen earlier. A common-envelope exponential T2star of about **3.0 us** is supported by raw signal, fitted-reference normalization, skip-transient analysis, model comparison, and block bootstrap. A conservative model/statistical interval is about **2.4-4.0 us**. - The two frequencies are centered near 1.89 MHz rather than the programmed 1.5 MHz, plausibly reflecting residual microwave/resonance-center offset. Their half-separation is about 0.37 MHz and is consistent, at this scan's resolution and bootstrap uncertainty, with the calculated 13C Larmor frequency of 0.3848 MHz. The 13C-spaced model loses essentially no fit quality relative to an unconstrained pair and is strongly preferred to a single tone. This is strong evidence for **13C-related Ramsey modulation/sidebands** on r03. - The raw/reference agreement, balanced snake-direction check, and phase coherence across all stored averages make random noise, denominator structure, or simple scan-direction drift unlikely explanations. A fixed control/timing spectral artifact is not excluded by this one run. ## Claims not yet supported - The data do not uniquely prove that the pair comes from one particular nearby 13C nucleus; they do not determine a hyperfine tensor, nuclear position, number of contributing nuclei, or polarization. - Do not quote T2star more precisely than roughly 3.0 us or treat the bootstrap interval as covering all model/systematic uncertainty. A stretched-exponential exponent is not established. - The fitted 1.89 MHz pair center is not a new sub-grid ODMR calibration, and the reason for its roughly +0.39 MHz offset from programmed detuning is not uniquely established. - Absence of additional resolved components is not evidence that no other 13C spins are present. ## Recommended next action Run one discriminating, refreshed-center long-span Ramsey at **det = 1.0 MHz** with the same 0.048-8.048 us grid and comparable shot budget. The physical-pair hypothesis predicts both components shift downward by about 0.5 MHz while retaining approximately 0.77 MHz separation and approximately 3 us decay (roughly 1.01 and 1.78 MHz given the present fitted offset). A fixed control/timing artifact would not translate this way. Use raw and fitted-reference views and the same block-bootstrap model comparison; this is a targeted confirmation, not another blind repeat.

The 8.0 µs span gives 0.125 MHz nominal Fourier resolution and the 0.2 µs sampling gives 2.5 MHz Nyquist. ## Calculations/scripts run - `analyze_ramsey.py` verified the raw axis contract `[scan, average, readout, point]` by reproducing both combined readouts from the 20 averages (maximum mismatch (1.4\times10^{-14}) kcps), compared raw signal, raw reference, signal/reference, and signal-reference, ran dense sinusoidal least-squares screens, first-four-point exclusion, odd/even-average checks, per-average complex-coefficient/SEM checks, a scan-order-aware common-mode drift check, and bootstrap damped-sinusoid fits. Outputs: `ramsey_analysis.json` and `ramsey_review_plot.png`. - `analyze_ramsey_doublet.py` compared damped single-frequency and two-frequency models for exponential and Gaussian envelopes, with free or predicted-13C splitting, and bootstrapped the 20 stored averages 500 times per analysis variant. Output: `ramsey_doublet_analysis.json`. - `ramsey_coherence_null.py` performed 5,000 independent circular-shift null trials, preserving each average's correlation structure while testing a coherent pair with the locally predicted 0.3848065 MHz half-splitting. Output: `ramsey_coherence_null.json`. Quantitative checks: - No average crossed the documented 15% acquisition-order common-mode drop threshold; the largest replicated drift score was 6.5%. Per-point median SEM was 0.850 kcps for raw signal and 0.0116 for signal/reference. - The strongest full-span component is 2.269 MHz in raw signal (0.817 kcps amplitude, (R^2=0.327) over a linear baseline) and 2.266 MHz in signal/reference (0.01853 amplitude, (R^2=0.347)); after omitting the first four points it remains at 2.273/2.265 MHz. The reference readout instead peaks near 0.808 MHz, arguing against simple reference leakage. - A second coherent signal component lies near 1.5 MHz. In signal/reference its per-average complex amplitude/SEM is 3.90 at 1.5 MHz; the 2.266 MHz component is 5.61. The planned low/high sideband targets treated individually give 0.79 and 2.43, respectively, so the original assumption of a carrier fixed exactly at 1.5 MHz is not the best description. - A free doublet fit to signal/reference gives center 1.896 MHz and half-splitting 0.369 MHz (lower/upper 1.527/2.265 MHz); excluding four early points gives center 1.884 MHz and half-splitting 0.374 MHz. This is close to the locally predicted 13C Larmor frequency, 0.3848065 MHz, at the run's 0.125 MHz nominal resolution. A model with that predicted split is strongly preferred descriptively over a single damped tone (signal/reference ΔBIC = -23 full span and -16 after skipping four points; raw-signal ΔBIC about -38 and -30). The search-corrected circular-shift test found the predicted-split pair at center 1.890 MHz with empirical (p=0.00020) (the 1/5001 floor). - Candidate-doublet decay fits are model/readout dependent: predicted-split fits give about 2.9–4.5 µs in raw signal and 3.8–5.3 µs in signal/reference or signal-reference as the envelope changes from exponential to Gaussian and early points are included/excluded. Bootstrap intervals are narrower within each model, so model choice/common-mode handling—not optimizer failure—is the main current uncertainty. ## Plausible interpretation This run contains a statistically coherent Ramsey signal, unlike the earlier non-claim-grade scouts. The most economical physical interpretation is a Ramsey doublet centered near 1.89 MHz, shifted from the programmed 1.5 MHz by a resonance-center offset that is still compatible with the broad/several-hundred-kHz pODMR calibration uncertainty. Its approximately 0.37 MHz half-splitting is consistent, within scan resolution and analysis systematics, with the predicted 0.3848 MHz nearby-13C scale. Under that model, T2* is a few microseconds, plausibly around 4 µs (roughly 3–5 µs across defensible analysis variants). ## Claims not yet supported - A precise single T2* value or a justified choice between exponential and Gaussian decay is not supported; quoting a sub-µs uncertainty would ignore readout/model/transient systematics. - A definitive nearby-13C assignment, number of coupled nuclei, or hyperfine tensor is not yet supported by one detuning setting. The lower component is close to the programmed 1.5 MHz phase frequency, so a coherent sequence/electronics contribution remains an alternative even though the component is absent from the reference readout. - The 2.27 MHz peak alone should not be called the bare Ramsey carrier, and the originally fixed 1.115/1.885 MHz target pair is not directly observed under the fixed-carrier assumption. ## Recommended next action Run one confirmatory, equally high-SNR long-span Ramsey with a deliberate detuning shift and phase cycling if available; `det=1.2 MHz` with the same 0.048–8.048 µs/0.2 µs sampling is a clean choice. The physical-doublet prediction is that both lines move down by about 0.3 MHz to roughly 1.20 and 1.97 MHz, retain a half-splitting near 0.385 MHz, and give a compatible few-µs decay. A component fixed at 1.5 MHz would instead identify sequence/electronics contamination. This single discriminating measurement is preferable to another identical repeat.

It used `mw_freq=3.8765 GHz`, programmed detuning 1.5 MHz, tau 0.048-8.048 us in 0.200 us steps (41 points), snake order, and 20 x 50,000 repetitions = 1.0 million shots per tau point. ## Calculations or scripts run - Created and ran `ramsey_analysis.py`; outputs are `ramsey_analysis_results.json` and `ramsey_analysis.png`. - Verified the raw array contract: `ExperimentDataEachAvg` is `[20 averages, 2 readouts, 41 tau points]` and its mean reproduces `ExperimentData`. - Compared raw signal, point-wise signal/reference, and signal divided by a fitted reference line. However, a single stationary damped Ramsey model is not supported: an almost equally strong approximately 2.28 MHz component competes with the carrier, the selected frequency changes with run half and scan direction, and the primary raw fit loses model preference when the first four points are removed. Inspect/validate and then run a phase-cycled differential or quadrature Ramsey measurement on r03, with an immediately refreshed pODMR center and complementary final-pulse phases interleaved at each tau. First use a dense short window (about 0.05-4 us with no more than 0.1 us spacing) to test whether a carrier and decay reproduce independently of scan direction and early baseline. Quantified common-mode behavior and reference-readout spectra. - Compared fixed-carrier, fixed-sideband, shifted symmetric-doublet/triplet models with AICc. Profiled common exponential and Gaussian decay envelopes and ran a 500-resample average-level bootstrap for a conditional T2star estimate. ## Plausible interpretation - This run contains a coherent Ramsey-like signal, unlike the earlier mixed scouts. The strongest single-frequency screen is 2.266-2.273 MHz, while the programmed 1.5 MHz component is also present. The global and carrier coherence screens give exploratory sign-flip p values of 0.00060 and 0.00020. Raw signal and both normalization views agree on the main structure; the combined reference trace does not carry the strong outer peaks. - A shifted symmetric model is more informative than treating 2.27 MHz as one carrier. Direction/time subsets give centers 1.884-1.896 MHz and spacings 0.360-0.384 MHz. This is close to the project-model 13C Larmor value 0.384806 MHz and is resolvable in the 8 us record. A shifted triplet gives 1.528/1.894/2.260 MHz and beats the best one-frequency model by about 16 AICc units in the point-wise-ratio view, but its center component is comparable to a reference-readout component; the signal-specific evidence is therefore cleaner as a doublet. - The inferred center is about 0.394 MHz above the programmed detuning, compatible with the stated several-100-kHz pODMR-center uncertainty. The spacing match and subset stability make 13C a strong candidate, but one high-SNR detuning condition cannot exclude a tau-periodic sequence/acquisition artifact or another spectral mechanism. - A unique, model-independent T2star with an unconditional confidence interval. Jointly fit both detunings using raw signal and fitted-reference-line normalization as primary views. Settings were `mw_freq=3.8765 GHz`, programmed `det=1.5 MHz`, `tau=0.048..8.048 us` in `0.2 us` steps, and `20 x 50000` shots per tau point. - Project context: `project/state.md`, `project/advice.md`, `md/memory.md`, `md/knowledge.md`, and `evidence/e001.json`, `e014.json`, and `e016.json`. These establish accepted candidate r03, the refreshed weak-pi pODMR center, readout/analysis conventions, and planned targets: carrier `1.5 MHz` and approximate 13C sidebands `1.115/1.885 MHz`. - The saved XML in `m001.json` confirms that with `full_experiment=0`, readout 1 is the true mS=0 reference and readout 2 is the Ramsey signal. ## Calculations/scripts run - `analyze_ramsey.py` generated `ramsey_analysis.json` and `ramsey_diagnostic.png`: raw signal, point-wise ratio, fitted-reference-line normalization, linear-baseline least-squares frequency screens, Hann-window FFT, target amplitudes, per-average/split consistency, empirical SEM, scan-order-aware drift slopes, and an average bootstrap. - Sampling checks: 8.0 us span gives `0.125 MHz` nominal resolution; 0.2 us spacing gives `2.5 MHz` Nyquist. - No average exceeded a 15% scan-order-aware common-mode decline. Mean common brightness nevertheless ranged `39.56..52.64 kcps` between tracked averages. - Full-span sinusoid screens prefer `2.266..2.269 MHz` in raw signal and both normalization views (raw amplitude `0.817 kcps`; ratio amplitude `0.0184`). The programmed `1.5 MHz` component is also present but smaller (raw `0.705 kcps`; ratio `0.0157`). Median point SEM is `0.850 kcps` raw and `0.0116` in ratio, so coherence across tau/averages matters more than a single point. - The FFT is analysis-sensitive: raw and fitted-reference-normalized peaks are near `2.281 MHz`, while the Hann-window ratio peak is near `1.516 MHz`. Split screens are likewise inconsistent: first 10 averages peak near `1.518 MHz`, last 10 near `2.265 MHz`; forward/odd and reverse/even undamped screens peak near `2.283` and `1.519 MHz` respectively. Per-average maxima range broadly from `0.351` to `2.330 MHz`. - `fit_ramsey.py` generated `ramsey_damped_fits.json`. Damped-sinusoid fits change branch with readout view, early-point omission, and exponential versus Gaussian envelope: representative fitted frequencies are about `1.53` or `2.24..2.28 MHz`, with descriptive `T2star` values about `1.8..5.2 us`. Average-bootstrap ratio fits are bimodal; for a full exponential fit the 95% intervals are `1.51..2.34 MHz` and `0.94..6.16 us`. These are robustness diagnostics, not parameter confidence intervals for an accepted physical model. ## Plausible interpretation - The run is healthy and analyzable. Raw signal, point-wise ratio, and fitted-reference normalization contain an early-time, phase-coherent oscillatory contrast across averages. A Ramsey response is plausible, with a characteristic decay of a few microseconds. - It is not a clean single-carrier Ramsey trace. The competition between approximately `1.5` and `2.27 MHz`, the strong dependence on windowing/early points, and split disagreement suggest a mixture of genuine Ramsey contrast with a tau-dependent transient, frequency-offset drift, alias-edge structure, or more than one spectral component. - At the pre-registered 13C targets, the full-span ratio amplitudes are only `0.0028` at `1.115 MHz` and `0.0096` at `1.885 MHz`, versus `0.0157` at the programmed carrier; the low/high targets do not form a consistent sideband pair. If `~2.27 MHz` is instead the actual carrier, its upper `+~0.385 MHz` sideband would lie near `2.65 MHz`, above the present Nyquist limit and alias into the measured band. Thus this dataset cannot cleanly adjudicate that shifted-carrier 13C model. ## Claims that are not yet supported - No claim-grade numerical `T2star`: the fitted value is model-, window-, and frequency-branch-dependent. Reporting only “descriptively a few microseconds” is appropriate. - No unique carrier frequency or calibrated resonance offset from this run. - No nearby-13C detection, coupling value, or exclusion. The planned `1.115/1.885 MHz` pair is inconsistent, and a shifted-carrier upper sideband is not unaliased by this sampling. - No assignment of the `~2.27 MHz` component to an NV carrier, a 13C sideband, or an instrumental transient without a discriminating measurement. ## Recommended next action Do not repeat this same Ramsey acquisition. First re-bracket the resonance with a short fine weak-pi pODMR immediately before acquisition, then use a complementary-phase/phase-cycled Ramsey readout so the paired difference suppresses the large common tau-dependent transient. Use an approximately 8 us span with `<=0.1 us` spacing (Nyquist `>=5 MHz`) and choose the programmed detuning from the fresh center so the carrier and both `carrier +/- ~0.385 MHz` targets are unaliased. Pre-register the carrier/sideband frequencies and require agreement between raw paired contrast, scan-direction splits, and stored averages before fitting `T2star` or assigning 13C.

The inspected active path says readout 1 is the mS=0 reference and readout 2 is the Ramsey signal for `full_experiment=0`. ## Calculations/scripts run - Ran `ramsey_analysis.py`; machine-readable results and the review plot are in `ramsey_analysis_results.json` and `ramsey_analysis.png`. - Checked raw readouts, point-wise signal/reference, normalization to a fitted reference line, stored-average behavior, and snake-order acquisition drift. - Ran detrended FFTs and linear-baseline sinusoid scans over 0.5-2.5 MHz. The actual N-point DFT spacing is 0.1613 MHz (the design value `1/span` is 0.1667 MHz); Nyquist is 2.5 MHz. - Fit exponential and Gaussian Ramsey envelopes, calculated formal covariance errors, enumerated all 256 average-level bootstrap resamples in the candidate basin, and checked leave-one-tau influence. - Ran 20,000-permutation look-elsewhere checks and compared one-component versus fixed +/-0.3846 MHz ^13C-sideband models by BIC. ## Plausible interpretation - The acquisition is usable: it completed without abort, final counts were 38.249 kcps (12.1% below the fresh 43.535 kcps track value but above the 20 kcps gate), and scan-order common-mode drop scores were 0%, 3.34%, 3.00%, and 0%; none reaches the local 15% drift flag. - The signal has a plausible Ramsey-like component at 0.962 MHz with 1.081 kcps sinusoidal amplitude (about 2.4% of the 45.32 kcps mean reference). Point-wise normalization gives 0.949 MHz; normalization to a fitted reference line gives the same result as raw signal. The reference-channel amplitude at 0.962 MHz is only 0.167 kcps. Fixed-frequency signal phases across averages are -167, -117, -166, and -173 degrees, although individual traces are noisy. - Evidence strength is mixed. A common-phase joint-average permutation check gives exploratory `p=0.012`, but a permutation check using only the final averaged trace gives `p=0.211` after scanning the full band. The defensible interpretation is therefore only that a few-microsecond decay is plausible. - The expected ^13C scale from the local model is 0.3846 MHz. Around the fitted 0.959 MHz carrier, expected side components would lie near 0.575 and 1.344 MHz. The FFT has no convincing paired feature, and adding both side components worsens BIC by +8.24 relative to one component despite reducing RSS. With a plausible 2-3 us envelope, the useful coherent record is also too short for a strong sideband exclusion. ## Claims not yet supported - No claim-grade numerical T2star is established; quoting either envelope fit as the result would hide model and resampling uncertainty. - Neither presence nor absence of a nearby ^13C is established. Isolated FFT bins, including the 1.29/1.61 MHz structure, should not be assigned to ^13C. - The approximately 0.96 MHz component is not yet proven to be a stable Ramsey carrier rather than a repeatable acquisition/systematic fluctuation. The prior r03 pODMR evidence still supports using r03; this scout does not invalidate alignment. ## Recommended next action Re-track r03, then make a sub-MHz-step narrow weak-pi pODMR center check around the prior 3.8763 GHz descriptive center before repeating Ramsey. Accept a T2star fit only if the raw and fitted-reference-normalized carrier recurs with consistent phase and the reference stays clean. Pursue a longer/higher-resolution ^13C Ramsey scan only after that carrier/T2star replication is secure.
